ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese dry vermicelli with charbroiled ribeye. FOODON_00002473 (beef noodle dish) is the closest parent category.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP covers beef noodle dishes broadly; no specific code exists for Vietnamese dry vermicelli with grilled ribeye steak.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Vietnamese noodle dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 450 g, comprising roughly 150 g cooked rice vermicelli, 120 g charbroiled ribeye steak, and the remainder as bean sprouts, fresh herbs, and dipping sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from USDA FoodData Central: Beef, ribeye steak, grilled (NDB 23634) and Rice vermicelli, cooked (NDB 20113), combined with HPB Singapore nutrient data for Vietnamese beef noodle dishes
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly: approximately 27% cooked ribeye steak (USDA NDB 23634), 33% cooked rice vermicelli (USDA NDB 20113), 15% bean sprouts (USDA NDB 11043), 10% fresh herbs and vegetables, and 15% dipping sauce / condiments. Sodium is elevated to reflect fish-sauce-based dipping sauce. Values are per 100 g of the assembled dish.

gi_source.title: Rice vermicelli GI reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Rice vermicelli GI = 53 (boiled).
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: The GI of this dish is estimated primarily from the rice vermicelli component (GI 53, boiled, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008). The protein and fat from ribeye steak lower the overall glycemic response. A composite GI of 53 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving = (53 x 74.25 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = approximately 39.4, rounded to 39.5.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Each 450 g serving provides approximately 1890 mg sodium, which represents 82–94% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced sauce, consume the dipping sauce sparingly, and limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice vermicelli carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: The dish has a GI of approximately 53 (low) but a high glycemic load of ~39.5 per full serving due to the large vermicelli portion; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of noodles, increase the protein-to-carbohydrate ratio, and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ribeye steak and elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ribeye steak contributes saturated fat (~1.9 g per 100 g) and the dish's sodium content is high; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit serving frequency, choose leaner cuts where available, and minimise dipping sauce consumption to reduce both saturated fat and sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Charbroiled Ribeye Steak Vermicelli (Dry) is a Vietnamese-inspired noodle dish served in Singapore, featuring a generous portion of charbroiled or chargrilled ribeye steak sliced and placed atop a bed of dry rice vermicelli (bún khô). Unlike soup-based Vietnamese noodle dishes, this dry version is served without broth; the noodles are tossed with a savoury fish-sauce-based dressing, garlic oil, and chilli, then topped with fresh bean sprouts, Vietnamese herbs such as perilla and mint, sliced cucumber, and fried shallots. The ribeye steak is marinated and cooked over high direct heat, producing a charred exterior with a tender, juicy interior and a smoky, umami-rich flavour. The dish is characterised by a contrast of textures: silky noodles, crisp bean sprouts, and caramelised beef. Nutritionally, it provides a moderate energy density of approximately 148 kcal per 100 g, with meaningful protein from the ribeye (approximately 10 g per 100 g of assembled dish) and carbohydrate primarily from rice vermicelli. The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low), though the glycemic load per full serving is high due to portion size. Sodium content is elevated due to fish sauce, making this dish a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. It is dairy-free and suitable for those avoiding lactose. The dish is popular in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore and is commonly ordered as a main meal for lunch or dinner.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component assembly using USDA FoodData Central data for individual ingredients (grilled ribeye steak, cooked rice vermicelli, bean sprouts). The GI is estimated from the rice vermicelli component per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved sources and the assembly methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from component assembly (USDA FoodData Central NDB 23634 grilled ribeye, NDB 20113 cooked rice vermicelli, NDB 11043 bean sprouts) weighted by approximate serving proportions. Sodium is elevated to reflect fish-sauce-based dressing typical of this dish style. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources for this assembled dish. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources for this assembled dish. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported with sufficient confidence across all components. GI of 53 is based on boiled rice vermicelli per Atkinson et al. 2008 (University of Sydney GI Database); the protein and fat from ribeye steak are expected to modestly lower the composite GI. Halal status is marked false as the restaurant (Viet'Spice) is not confirmed halal-certified. The dish is served at Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt, Circular Road, Singapore, at SGD 29.90 per serving.
